title: Original Legacy Bar Multiple-Choice Drafts - Batch 001
description: Twenty-one original, AI-assisted draft questions: three per legacy MBE subject. These are not NCBE questions and are not endorsed by NCBE.

rights.marketingRestriction: Do not call these official, released, licensed, or actual MBE questions. Do not imply NCBE affiliation or endorsement.

styleNotes: Use a realistic factual scenario, a distinct call of the question, and four parallel options.
Test application of one principal rule, occasionally with one closely related rule.
Avoid trivia, local law, obscure exceptions, trick wording, and irrelevant cultural knowledge.
Each distractor must reflect a plausible legal error and must be independently explained.
Keep party labels neutral and facts no more detailed than needed to resolve the issue.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.stem: A corporation is incorporated and has its principal place of business in State A. It manufactures specialized climbing harnesses and sells them through its website. During the past three years, the corporation has shipped 60 harnesses to customers in State B and has purchased online advertisements directed specifically to residents of State B. A State B resident bought one of the harnesses through the website. While the resident was using the harness in State B, a buckle allegedly failed and caused serious injuries. The resident sued the corporation in a state court in State B. The corporation has no office, employee, or bank account in State B and moved to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction.
questions.call: Should the court grant the corporation's motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the corporation is neither incorporated nor headquartered in State B.
choices.rationale: This addresses general jurisdiction, but the resident relies on specific jurisdiction tied to the corporation's forum-directed sales and the forum injury.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because a corporation must have a physical presence in a state before that state's courts may exercise jurisdiction.
choices.rationale: Physical presence is not required when the defendant purposefully directs relevant commercial activity toward the forum.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the corporation purposefully sold the harness in State B and the claim arises from that forum contact.
choices.rationale: This correctly identifies purposeful forum contacts and the required connection between those contacts and the claim.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because operating any website accessible in State B subjects the corporation to jurisdiction there for every claim.
choices.rationale: A universally accessible website alone does not create jurisdiction for every claim; purposeful forum activity and relatedness are required.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether State B may exercise specific personal jurisdiction over an out-of-state seller.
explanation.governingRule: Specific jurisdiction generally requires purposeful contacts with the forum, a claim that arises out of or relates to those contacts, and an exercise of jurisdiction consistent with fair play and substantial justice.
explanation.application: The corporation deliberately cultivated State B customers, sold the particular product to a State B resident, and the alleged injury occurred there from that sale. Those facts support specific jurisdiction even though the corporation is not at home in State B.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating the absence of an office or headquarters as dispositive. Those facts matter primarily to general jurisdiction.
explanation.memoryAid: Specific jurisdiction asks: purposeful forum contact plus a related claim.

authorityNotes: Ford Motor Co. v. Montana Eighth Judicial District Court

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.stem: A citizen of State A sued a citizen of State B in a federal district court in State B, invoking diversity jurisdiction. The complaint asserted a fraud claim created by State B law. Under a statute of State B, a plaintiff must prove each element of fraud by clear and convincing evidence. In ordinary federal civil actions, a party generally must prove disputed facts by a preponderance of the evidence. The parties agree that no Federal Rule of Civil Procedure directly addresses the burden of persuasion for this claim. Before trial, the plaintiff asked the federal court to instruct the jury to apply the federal preponderance standard.
questions.call: Which burden of persuasion should the federal court apply?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The federal preponderance standard, because burdens of persuasion are always procedural.
choices.rationale: Burdens of persuasion governing elements of a state-created claim are generally treated as substantive for Erie purposes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The State B clear-and-convincing standard, because it is part of the substantive law governing the state claim.
choices.rationale: This correctly applies state substantive law in a diversity case when no controlling federal rule conflicts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The federal preponderance standard, because federal courts use one uniform standard in all civil jury trials.
choices.rationale: Federal courts do not disregard state-law burdens that form part of the governing substantive claim.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whichever standard the court concludes would produce the fairer result in this case.
choices.rationale: Erie analysis does not permit ad hoc selection based on a judge's view of fairness.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a federal diversity court applies a state-law burden of persuasion to a state-created claim.
explanation.governingRule: A federal court sitting in diversity applies federal procedural law and the forum state's substantive law. A state burden of persuasion attached to a state-law claim is ordinarily substantive when no valid federal rule directly controls.
explanation.application: The claim arises under State B law, and State B has made the heightened burden part of that claim. The federal court should therefore use the clear-and-convincing standard.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming every matter concerning trial mechanics is procedural. Erie classification depends on the function and effect of the rule.
explanation.memoryAid: State claim, state elements, state burden - unless controlling federal law says otherwise.

authorityNotes: Federal diversity doctrine concerning state-law burdens of persuasion

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.stem: Immediately after an accident at a warehouse, the warehouse owner's lawyer directed an investigator to interview the only eyewitness. The investigator prepared a memorandum containing a nearly verbatim account of the eyewitness's observations followed by the investigator's impressions about the eyewitness's credibility and suggestions for trial strategy. The injured worker later filed a federal action against the owner. By then, the eyewitness had moved to a country beyond the court's subpoena power and had repeatedly refused to speak with either party. The worker requested production of the investigator's entire memorandum. The owner objected on work-product grounds.
questions.call: How should the court rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Order production of the entire memorandum, because the eyewitness's observations are relevant to the action.
choices.rationale: Relevance alone does not overcome work-product protection, and opinion work product receives especially strong protection.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deny production of the entire memorandum, because materials prepared in anticipation of litigation are never discoverable.
choices.rationale: Ordinary factual work product may be discovered upon substantial need and inability to obtain the substantial equivalent without undue hardship.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deny production unless the worker first proves that the owner's lawyer acted in bad faith.
choices.rationale: Bad faith is not the governing standard for overcoming ordinary work-product protection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Order production of the factual account but protect the investigator's impressions and strategy from disclosure.
choices.rationale: The unavailable eyewitness creates substantial need and undue hardship, while mental impressions and strategy should remain protected.

explanation.testedIssue: The extent to which factual and opinion work product may be discovered.
explanation.governingRule: Documents prepared in anticipation of litigation are protected. A court may order production of factual work product upon substantial need and undue hardship, but it must protect mental impressions, conclusions, opinions, and legal theories.
explanation.application: Because the sole eyewitness is unavailable, the worker has a strong need for the factual account and cannot obtain an equivalent. The investigator's credibility judgments and strategic suggestions remain protected.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating all work product as either fully discoverable or absolutely immune.
explanation.memoryAid: Facts may yield to need; legal theories and mental impressions receive stronger protection.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.category: The relation of nation and states in a federal system
questions.stem: A state requires milk produced outside the state to be pasteurized at a facility located within the state before the milk may be sold there. Milk produced within the state may be pasteurized at any facility that meets the same health standards, including facilities outside the state. The state asserts that the requirement makes inspection more convenient. An out-of-state dairy uses a federally inspected pasteurization facility located 10 miles from the state border. Complying with the state law would require the dairy to transport its milk an additional 180 miles to an in-state facility. The dairy challenges the law.
questions.call: Is the law likely constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the law discriminates against interstate commerce and less discriminatory inspection methods appear available.
choices.rationale: This correctly applies the stringent review used for facially discriminatory state economic regulation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because protecting public health is within the state's police power.
choices.rationale: A legitimate objective does not by itself validate a law that facially discriminates against interstate commerce.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, if the law's local benefits are greater than the burden imposed on interstate commerce.
choices.rationale: Ordinary balancing is not the primary test for facial discrimination; discriminatory laws face a much more demanding standard.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only if Congress has enacted a federal statute regulating milk pasteurization.
choices.rationale: The dormant Commerce Clause may invalidate discriminatory state laws even without conflicting federal legislation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a facially discriminatory state law violates the dormant Commerce Clause.
explanation.governingRule: A state law that discriminates on its face against interstate commerce is generally invalid unless the state shows that it serves an important local purpose that cannot be adequately served by reasonable nondiscriminatory alternatives.
explanation.application: The law imposes a location requirement only on out-of-state milk. Inspection, certification, or reciprocal enforcement could likely serve the health objective without requiring in-state processing.
explanation.commonTrap: Using the more deferential balancing test for a law that is facially neutral. Facial discrimination triggers more exacting review.
explanation.memoryAid: Discrimination first; balancing second only for neutral burdens.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.stem: Congress enacted a statute authorizing a federal agency to issue binding workplace-safety regulations. The statute also provided that either house of Congress could invalidate any regulation within 60 days by adopting a resolution. A resolution under the statute would not be presented to the other house or to the president. The agency issued a regulation limiting employee exposure to a hazardous chemical. Forty days later, the House of Representatives adopted a resolution invalidating the regulation. An affected employee challenged the House's action.
questions.call: Is the House's action constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because Congress may retain control over authority that it has delegated to an agency.
choices.rationale: Congress may conduct oversight, but action changing legal rights must comply with the Constitution's lawmaking procedures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because only one house is required to act when the original statute expressly authorizes that procedure.
choices.rationale: A statute cannot dispense with constitutional bicameralism and presentment requirements for legislative action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because invalidating the regulation is legislative action that must satisfy bicameralism and presentment.
choices.rationale: This is the governing constitutional defect in a one-house legislative veto.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because only a federal court may invalidate an agency regulation.
choices.rationale: Congress may alter or repeal regulatory authority through valid legislation; judicial review is not the exclusive means of changing a rule.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a one-house legislative veto complies with the Constitution.
explanation.governingRule: Congressional action that changes legal rights or obligations is legislative in character and generally must be approved by both houses and presented to the president.
explanation.application: The House resolution purported to extinguish a binding rule without Senate approval or presidential presentment, so the procedure is unconstitutional.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming Congress may reserve any review mechanism it writes into the original delegation.
explanation.memoryAid: If Congress changes the law, think bicameralism plus presentment.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: First Amendment - designated public forum and viewpoint discrimination
questions.stem: A city owns an auditorium that it makes available, on a first-come basis, to nonprofit civic organizations for evening meetings. The city has permitted organizations to discuss taxes, public education, policing, and environmental policy. An organization applied to reserve the auditorium for a meeting advocating expanded legal protections for recent immigrants. The auditorium was available, and the organization satisfied every written eligibility requirement. City officials denied the application solely because they believed the organization's position would be controversial and would provoke criticism of the city government.
questions.call: Was the denial constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the city owns the auditorium and may choose which viewpoints appear there.
choices.rationale: Government ownership does not permit viewpoint discrimination after the government opens property as a forum for civic expression.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the city opened the auditorium for civic discussion and excluded the organization because of its viewpoint.
choices.rationale: The exclusion is viewpoint based and is presumptively unconstitutional in the forum the city created.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because avoiding controversy is a reasonable basis for denying access to government property.
choices.rationale: Avoiding criticism or controversy is not a valid basis for viewpoint discrimination in a designated public forum.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because every government-owned building is automatically a traditional public forum.
choices.rationale: Government buildings are not automatically traditional public forums; the result follows from the city's designated use and viewpoint discrimination.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a city may exclude a speaker from a designated public forum because officials disapprove of the speaker's viewpoint.
explanation.governingRule: When government opens property for expressive activity by a defined class of speakers, it may enforce reasonable, viewpoint-neutral limits consistent with the forum's purpose, but it may not exclude speech because of viewpoint.
explanation.application: The organization fit the forum's eligibility rules, and the city denied access solely because of the position it planned to advocate. That is viewpoint discrimination.
explanation.commonTrap: Concluding that government ownership alone gives officials unlimited editorial control.
explanation.memoryAid: Once government opens a forum, eligibility rules may be neutral; viewpoints may not be favored or disfavored.

authorityNotes: Perry Education Association v. Perry Local Educators' Association
Rosenberger v. Rector and Visitors of the University of Virginia

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.stem: A homeowner delivered a signed writing to several painters stating, 'I will pay $8,000 to anyone who completely repaints my house by June 1. Acceptance may be made only by complete performance.' On May 10, after reading the writing, a painter began the work. By May 16, the painter had completed half of the house. That morning, the homeowner told the painter that the offer was revoked and directed the painter to leave. The painter was ready and able to finish the work by June 1.
questions.call: What is the painter's strongest argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The homeowner could not revoke the offer because the signed writing was automatically an option contract.
choices.rationale: A signed offer is not an option contract without consideration or another recognized basis for irrevocability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The painter accepted the offer and formed a bilateral contract immediately upon beginning the work.
choices.rationale: The offer expressly invited acceptance only by complete performance, so beginning performance did not itself constitute acceptance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The revocation was effective because an offer for a unilateral contract remains revocable until performance is complete.
choices.rationale: That was the older rule; the modern approach protects an offeree who has begun the invited performance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beginning the invited performance created an option contract that made the offer temporarily irrevocable.
choices.rationale: Under the modern rule, beginning performance on a unilateral offer creates an option contract, while acceptance still requires completion.

explanation.testedIssue: The effect of beginning performance in response to an offer inviting acceptance only by complete performance.
explanation.governingRule: When an offer invites acceptance by performance only, the beginning of the invited performance creates an option contract that makes the offer temporarily irrevocable. The offeror's duty to pay remains conditional on completion.
explanation.application: The painter began the requested performance and could timely complete it. The homeowner therefore could not revoke while the painter had a reasonable opportunity to finish.
explanation.commonTrap: Calling the relationship a bilateral contract as soon as performance starts. The better analysis is an option contract protecting the opportunity to complete.
explanation.memoryAid: Start performance: option arises. Finish performance: acceptance occurs.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.stem: A dealer and a collector entered into a written contract for the sale of a ceramic vase for $50,000. Both parties reasonably believed, based on a certificate supplied by an independent expert, that the vase had been made by a renowned artist in the eighteenth century. The contract did not assign either party the risk that the certificate might be inaccurate and did not contain an 'as is' clause. Shortly after delivery and payment, newly discovered laboratory testing established that the vase was a modern reproduction worth approximately $500. Neither party had reason to suspect the error when they contracted.
questions.call: May the collector avoid the contract?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a mistake concerning the value of the subject matter is never grounds for avoidance.
choices.rationale: The mistake concerned authenticity, an existing fact central to the exchange, not merely a later change in market value.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because both parties were mistaken about a basic assumption that materially affected the exchange.
choices.rationale: The mutual mistake concerned authenticity, materially altered the agreed exchange, and was not allocated to the collector.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the collector proves that the dealer knowingly misrepresented the vase's origin.
choices.rationale: Fraud is unnecessary when the requirements for mutual mistake are satisfied.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because title to the vase passed when the dealer delivered it.
choices.rationale: Transfer of title does not prevent avoidance when a valid contract defense such as mutual mistake applies.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a contract is voidable for mutual mistake about an existing fact central to the bargain.
explanation.governingRule: A contract is generally voidable by the adversely affected party when both parties were mistaken about a basic assumption, the mistake materially affects the exchange, and the party seeking relief did not bear the risk of the mistake.
explanation.application: Both parties shared a reasonable but false belief about the vase's authenticity, which was central to the price. The contract did not allocate that risk to the collector.
explanation.commonTrap: Labeling every mistake that influences price as a nonactionable mistake of value.
explanation.memoryAid: Ask whether the mistake concerns the thing's identity or essential qualities, not merely a market fluctuation.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.stem: A retailer entered into a contract with a lamp manufacturer for 200 black desk lamps to be delivered no later than September 30. The contract did not limit the manufacturer's right to cure. On September 20, the manufacturer delivered 200 otherwise conforming white lamps. The retailer immediately rejected the shipment and promptly notified the manufacturer. On September 21, the manufacturer informed the retailer that it would deliver 200 conforming black lamps on September 28. The manufacturer had the conforming lamps available and could make the promised delivery.
questions.call: Is the manufacturer entitled to cure the nonconforming delivery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the time for performance had not expired and the manufacturer seasonably notified the retailer of its intent to cure.
choices.rationale: This states the UCC cure rule for a nonconforming tender made before the contract deadline.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the retailer's proper rejection immediately terminated the manufacturer's right to perform.
choices.rationale: A seller may cure a nonconforming tender when the contract time has not yet expired and the seller gives seasonable notice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because cure is available only when the seller reasonably believed the first tender would be acceptable.
choices.rationale: Reasonable grounds are especially relevant to cure after the performance time; before the deadline, timely notice and conforming tender are enough.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the retailer cannot prove that the first delivery caused incidental damages.
choices.rationale: Possible damages do not eliminate the seller's timely statutory right to cure.

explanation.testedIssue: A seller's right to cure a nonconforming tender before the contract time expires.
explanation.governingRule: Under the UCC, when a seller makes a nonconforming tender and the time for performance has not expired, the seller may seasonably notify the buyer of an intent to cure and make a conforming delivery within the contract time.
explanation.application: The first delivery occurred 10 days before the deadline, and the manufacturer promptly proposed a conforming delivery before September 30. It may cure.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming perfect tender means one nonconforming delivery always ends the seller's performance rights.
explanation.memoryAid: Perfect tender permits rejection; timely cure may still save the sale.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.stem: A driver became angry when a street festival delayed traffic. Although police had closed a plaza to vehicles and hundreds of pedestrians were walking through it, the driver moved a barrier and drove through the plaza at 70 miles per hour to frighten the crowd and force people out of the way. The driver did not intend to strike anyone. Several pedestrians narrowly escaped, but the car struck and killed one person. The jurisdiction defines homicide offenses according to the common law.
questions.call: Of which homicide offense is the driver most likely guilty?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Involuntary manslaughter, because the driver did not intend to kill or injure anyone.
choices.rationale: Extremely reckless conduct showing conscious disregard for human life can supply malice even without an intent to kill.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Voluntary manslaughter, because the driver acted while angry.
choices.rationale: Ordinary anger caused by traffic delay is not adequate provocation, and the conduct is better analyzed as extreme recklessness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Murder, because driving through the crowd demonstrated a reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life.
choices.rationale: The facts support depraved-heart malice and therefore common-law murder.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No homicide offense, because the death was accidental from the driver's perspective.
choices.rationale: An unintended death may still be murder or manslaughter when caused with the required level of recklessness or negligence.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether extreme recklessness constitutes malice for common-law murder.
explanation.governingRule: Common-law murder includes an unintended killing caused by conduct demonstrating reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life, often called depraved-heart murder.
explanation.application: The driver knowingly created a grave and obvious risk by speeding through a densely occupied pedestrian plaza merely to frighten people. A jury could find malice.
explanation.commonTrap: Equating lack of purpose to kill with lack of malice.
explanation.memoryAid: Malice can be intent to kill, intent to seriously injure, felony murder, or depraved-heart recklessness.

authorityNotes: Common-law homicide doctrine; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.stem: A person agreed to help an acquaintance burglarize an office. The person gave the acquaintance a copied building key and a floor plan showing the location of a safe, intending that the acquaintance use them in the burglary. Several hours before the planned entry, the person regretted the agreement and sent the acquaintance a text message stating, 'I am out. Do not contact me again.' The person did not ask for the key or floor plan to be returned, notify the building owner, or contact the police. That night, the acquaintance used the key and floor plan to enter the office and steal money. The jurisdiction follows common-law principles.
questions.call: Is the person guilty of burglary as an accomplice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the person communicated a withdrawal before the acquaintance entered the office.
choices.rationale: After providing material assistance, effective withdrawal generally requires neutralizing the aid or timely warning authorities, not merely announcing withdrawal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because an accomplice must be physically present when the principal commits the crime.
choices.rationale: Physical presence is unnecessary when the defendant intentionally aids or encourages the crime.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the person received a share of the stolen money.
choices.rationale: Compensation or sharing in proceeds is not required for accomplice liability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the person intentionally aided the burglary and did not neutralize that assistance or give a timely warning.
choices.rationale: The key and floor plan materially assisted the crime, and the attempted withdrawal was ineffective.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an accomplice effectively withdrew after providing material assistance.
explanation.governingRule: A person who intentionally aids a crime is liable as an accomplice. To withdraw after providing assistance, the person generally must neutralize the aid or take timely steps, such as warning law enforcement, to prevent the crime.
explanation.application: The person supplied tools used in the burglary. A text renouncing the plan did not retrieve the tools, neutralize their value, or alert anyone capable of preventing the offense.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming verbal withdrawal is always enough, even after the actor has already supplied assistance.
explanation.memoryAid: Words may undo encouragement; material aid usually must be neutralized or counteracted.

authorityNotes: Common-law accomplice-liability doctrine; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Search incident to arrest - digital phone contents
questions.stem: Police officers had probable cause to arrest a suspect for a street robbery and lawfully arrested the suspect on a public sidewalk. During a search incident to the arrest, an officer removed a smartphone from the suspect's pocket and secured it. The officer had no reason to believe that the phone posed a physical danger or that its data would be destroyed before a warrant could be obtained. Without obtaining a warrant, the officer opened the phone's photo and message applications and found evidence linking the suspect to the robbery. The suspect moved to suppress the digital evidence.
questions.call: Should the court grant the motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because officers may search every item found on an arrestee's person without a warrant.
choices.rationale: The search-incident exception does not ordinarily permit examination of digital phone contents merely because the phone was seized from the arrestee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because absent exigent circumstances, police generally need a warrant to search digital information on a seized phone.
choices.rationale: This is the governing rule for digital phone contents after a lawful arrest.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because probable cause to arrest automatically supplies probable cause to search the phone's contents.
choices.rationale: Probable cause to arrest does not eliminate the warrant requirement for a separate digital search.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the officers were not permitted to seize the phone while making the arrest.
choices.rationale: The officers could seize and secure the phone; the constitutional problem was searching its digital contents without a warrant or exigency.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether police may search the digital contents of a phone incident to arrest without a warrant.
explanation.governingRule: Police may seize and secure a phone found on an arrestee, but they generally must obtain a warrant before searching its digital contents unless an established exception, such as exigent circumstances, applies.
explanation.application: The phone was secured, and the facts identify no safety concern or imminent destruction of data. The warrantless digital search was therefore invalid.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating a phone like a wallet or physical container for purposes of the search-incident doctrine.
explanation.memoryAid: Seize now, search digital data after a warrant - absent exigency.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Relevancy and reasons for excluding relevant evidence
questions.subtopic: Victim character in a self-defense case
questions.stem: A defendant is being tried in federal court for assaulting a patron outside a restaurant. The defendant claims self-defense and testifies that the patron attacked first. The defendant had never met the patron before the incident. The defendant calls a neighbor of the patron, who is prepared to testify that the patron has a reputation in the community for violence. The prosecution objects that the testimony is impermissible propensity evidence and is irrelevant because the defendant did not know of the patron's reputation at the time of the incident.
questions.call: How should the court rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Admit the testimony, because a criminal defendant may offer reputation evidence of the alleged victim's violent character to support self-defense.
choices.rationale: This is a permitted method of proving a pertinent victim trait, including to support an inference that the victim was the first aggressor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exclude the testimony, because character evidence may never be used to show that a person acted in conformity with a trait.
choices.rationale: The Federal Rules recognize specific exceptions, including a criminal defendant's use of a pertinent trait of an alleged victim.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Admit the testimony only if the neighbor describes specific violent acts committed by the patron.
choices.rationale: When character evidence is admissible, reputation or opinion is generally the permitted method on direct examination; specific acts are not required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exclude the testimony unless the patron first testifies as a prosecution witness.
choices.rationale: The exception does not depend on the alleged victim taking the stand.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a criminal defendant claiming self-defense may introduce reputation evidence of an alleged victim's violent character.
explanation.governingRule: A criminal defendant may offer evidence of a pertinent trait of the alleged victim. On direct examination, character is ordinarily proved by reputation or opinion testimony. Knowledge of the trait is not required when the evidence is used to suggest the victim was the first aggressor.
explanation.application: Violence is pertinent to the self-defense claim, and the neighbor proposes reputation testimony, an accepted method of proof.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing use of victim character to show first aggression with use of known violent acts to show the defendant's reasonable fear.
explanation.memoryAid: First aggressor: victim's reputation may matter even if unknown to the defendant. Reasonable fear: the defendant's prior knowledge matters.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
questions.subtopic: Business records and hearsay within hearsay
questions.stem: After a delivery truck collided with a cyclist, the delivery company's dispatcher prepared an incident report as part of the company's regular reporting system. The dispatcher had no personal knowledge of the collision. The report accurately recorded a telephone statement from an unidentified bystander who said that the truck had entered the intersection against a red light. At the cyclist's federal civil trial, the cyclist offers the report to prove that the truck entered on red. The cyclist establishes that the dispatcher routinely made such reports at or near the time of reported incidents, but offers no separate hearsay exception for the bystander's statement.
questions.call: Is the statement about the red light admissible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the report satisfies the business-records exception.
choices.rationale: The business-record foundation may cover the company's recordkeeping, but it does not automatically cure an outsider's hearsay statement embedded in the record.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the dispatcher accurately wrote down what the bystander said.
choices.rationale: Accurate transcription does not create a hearsay exception for the bystander's assertion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the bystander's statement is a separate hearsay layer that lacks its own exception.
choices.rationale: Each hearsay layer must be independently admissible, and the unidentified bystander was not shown to be acting under a business duty or within another exception.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because an employee who lacks personal knowledge can never create an admissible business record.
choices.rationale: A business record may be based on information transmitted by another person with a business duty; the problem here is the outsider's unsupported statement.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an outsider's statement embedded in a business record is admissible.
explanation.governingRule: Hearsay within hearsay is admissible only if each layer fits an exclusion or exception. A business record may satisfy one layer, but information supplied by an outsider who had no business duty requires an independent basis for admission.
explanation.application: The dispatcher made a regular record, but the bystander's assertion is the proposition offered for truth. No exception is supplied for that assertion.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming the business-record exception automatically covers everything written in a qualifying document.
explanation.memoryAid: A business record can contain two speakers; check an exception for each one.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.stem: A patron was injured when a loose handrail gave way on a stairway leading from a theater lobby to the sidewalk. The patron sued the theater in federal court for negligence. The theater denied that it owned or controlled the stairway and asserted that the building's landlord alone was responsible for it. One day after the accident, the theater's maintenance employees repaired and reinforced the handrail. The patron offers evidence of the repair solely to prove that the theater controlled the stairway. The theater objects under the rule excluding subsequent remedial measures.
questions.call: How should the court rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exclude the evidence, because subsequent remedial measures are inadmissible for every purpose.
choices.rationale: The rule bars certain uses, especially proving negligence or defect, but expressly permits other relevant uses such as disputed ownership or control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Admit the evidence to prove that the theater was negligent in failing to repair the handrail earlier.
choices.rationale: Using the repair to prove negligence is the prohibited propensity-like inference addressed by the rule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Admit the evidence only if a theater employee first denies that the repair occurred.
choices.rationale: Impeachment is another possible use, but it is not required when the evidence is independently admissible to prove disputed control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Admit the evidence for the limited purpose of proving control, because control is disputed.
choices.rationale: The Federal Rules permit this use when ownership or control is genuinely disputed.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether evidence of a subsequent repair may be used to prove disputed control.
explanation.governingRule: Subsequent remedial measures are not admissible to prove negligence, culpable conduct, a defect, or a need for warning. The court may admit them for another purpose, including proving ownership or control when disputed.
explanation.application: The theater placed control directly at issue, and the patron offers the repair only on that issue. A limiting instruction may confine the jury's use.
explanation.commonTrap: Reading the exclusion as absolute rather than purpose specific.
explanation.memoryAid: Ask why the evidence is offered: negligence is barred; disputed control may be allowed.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.stem: An owner conveyed a farm 'to a tenant for life, then to the owner's niece.' No minerals had ever been removed from the farm, and there were no open mines or quarries on the property. Five years later, the tenant discovered a valuable deposit of gravel beneath an unused field. Without the niece's consent, the tenant opened a large quarry, removed substantial quantities of gravel, and sold the gravel for profit. The operations permanently altered the field. The niece sued to stop further extraction and to recover the tenant's net profits.
questions.call: Is the niece likely to prevail?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a life tenant may make any profitable use of the land during the life tenancy.
choices.rationale: A life tenant must avoid affirmative waste that materially damages the future interest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because opening a new quarry and removing the gravel constitute affirmative waste.
choices.rationale: A life tenant generally may not begin new extraction that consumes natural resources when no open-mine exception applies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the tenant discovered the gravel after the life estate was created.
choices.rationale: Discovery timing does not authorize opening a new extraction operation that depletes the corpus of the property.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but the niece may obtain relief only after the tenant's death.
choices.rationale: A future-interest holder may seek present injunctive and monetary relief for ongoing waste.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a life tenant commits affirmative waste by opening a new quarry.
explanation.governingRule: A life tenant may use the property reasonably but may not commit affirmative waste by exploiting natural resources in a manner that materially reduces the property's value to future-interest holders. The open-mines doctrine may permit continued extraction from an existing operation, but not the opening of a new one absent authorization.
explanation.application: There was no existing quarry. The tenant created a new operation that depleted the land's substance and permanently altered the field, supporting injunction and accounting.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming possession during the life estate includes the unrestricted right to consume the property's natural resources.
explanation.memoryAid: Life tenant may enjoy the land, not consume the remainderman's inheritance.

authorityNotes: Common-law waste and open-mines doctrine; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.stem: An owner held two adjoining lots. For 10 years, the owner used a paved driveway across Lot 1 to reach a garage on Lot 2. The driveway was visible, regularly used, and substantially more practical than constructing access across a steep embankment on the other side of Lot 2. The owner then sold Lot 2 to a buyer by a deed that did not mention the driveway. The owner retained Lot 1 and immediately blocked the driveway. The buyer had observed the driveway before purchasing Lot 2 and demanded continued use.
questions.call: Does the buyer have an easement over Lot 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the apparent, continuous prior use was reasonably necessary to the enjoyment of Lot 2 when the parcels were severed.
choices.rationale: These facts support an easement implied from existing use upon severance of commonly owned land.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because every easement must be created by an express writing.
choices.rationale: Easements may arise by implication, necessity, prescription, or estoppel as well as by express grant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because Lot 2 is not completely landlocked.
choices.rationale: Strict necessity is not required for an easement implied from prior use; reasonable necessity may suffice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the owner's use of the driveway for 10 years created a prescriptive easement against the owner's own Lot 1.
choices.rationale: A person cannot acquire a prescriptive easement against property that the person owns; the relevant doctrine is implication upon severance.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a conveyance creates an easement implied from prior use.
explanation.governingRule: An easement may be implied when commonly owned land is severed and, before severance, one part was used for the benefit of another in a manner that was apparent, continuous, and reasonably necessary to the benefited parcel.
explanation.application: The long-standing visible driveway served Lot 2 while both lots were commonly owned and remained reasonably necessary when Lot 2 was sold.
explanation.commonTrap: Demanding absolute necessity or treating the owner's earlier use as adverse prescription.
explanation.memoryAid: Prior use easement: common ownership, severance, apparent continuous use, reasonable necessity.

authorityNotes: Common-law easement implied from prior use; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.stem: An owner conveyed land to a buyer, who paid value but did not record the deed. The owner later conveyed the same land to a second buyer for value. At the time of the second conveyance, the second buyer had no actual, record, or inquiry notice of the first conveyance. The first buyer recorded one day after the second conveyance. The second buyer recorded one week later. The jurisdiction's recording statute provides: 'No conveyance of land is valid against a subsequent purchaser for value without notice unless the conveyance is recorded.' Neither buyer is in possession.
questions.call: Who has superior title?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The first buyer, because the owner conveyed to that buyer first.
choices.rationale: The recording act protects a qualifying subsequent purchaser from a prior unrecorded conveyance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The first buyer, because that buyer recorded before the second buyer.
choices.rationale: A notice statute does not require the later bona fide purchaser to record first.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The second buyer, because that buyer paid value and lacked notice when the second conveyance occurred.
choices.rationale: Under a notice statute, the later bona fide purchaser prevails upon taking the conveyance without notice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The second buyer, but only because that buyer eventually recorded the deed.
choices.rationale: Recording is not an additional condition for protection under the quoted notice statute.

explanation.testedIssue: Priority under a notice recording statute.
explanation.governingRule: A notice statute protects a subsequent purchaser who gives value and lacks notice of the prior interest at the time of the later conveyance. The subsequent purchaser need not record before the earlier grantee.
explanation.application: The second buyer was a bona fide purchaser without notice when the deed was delivered, so the second buyer obtained priority despite recording after the first buyer.
explanation.commonTrap: Importing the first-to-record requirement of a race-notice statute into a pure notice statute.
explanation.memoryAid: Notice statute: later buyer wins at conveyance if value plus no notice.

authorityNotes: Recording-act doctrine - notice statute; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.stem: During an argument in a park, a person picked up a walking stick and swung it close to another person's face, intending only to make the other person fear an immediate blow. The person did not intend to make physical contact. The other person ducked, and the stick instead struck a nearby jogger, causing a broken nose. The jogger had not seen the stick before being struck. The jurisdiction follows traditional common-law rules for intentional torts.
questions.call: Is the person liable to the jogger for battery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the person did not intend to make contact with the jogger.
choices.rationale: Intent to commit assault against the intended target may transfer when the act instead causes a battery to another person.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, but the person may be liable for negligence if the swing was unreasonable.
choices.rationale: Negligence is not the only possible theory because the original assault intent transfers to the resulting battery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the jogger saw the stick and feared being struck before contact occurred.
choices.rationale: Awareness is required for assault, not battery. Harmful or offensive contact is sufficient for battery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the intent to commit an assault transfers to the harmful contact with the jogger.
choices.rationale: Traditional transferred-intent doctrine applies among assault, battery, false imprisonment, trespass to land, and trespass to chattels.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether intent to assault one person transfers when the act causes a battery to another.
explanation.governingRule: Under transferred intent, intent to commit certain traditional intentional torts against one person transfers when the act instead commits one of those torts against another person.
explanation.application: The actor intended to cause immediate apprehension in the original target and instead caused harmful contact with the jogger. The assault intent transfers to battery.
explanation.commonTrap: Requiring the actor to desire contact with the actual plaintiff.
explanation.memoryAid: Intent follows the act from the intended tort or person to the resulting traditional tort or victim.

authorityNotes: Common-law transferred-intent doctrine; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.stem: A hiker encountered an injured stranger lying safely beside a trail. The hiker had not caused the injury and had no special relationship with the stranger. The hiker nevertheless offered to carry the stranger to a nearby shelter. While doing so, the hiker carelessly failed to secure the stranger on a makeshift stretcher, causing the stranger to fall and suffer an additional injury. The jurisdiction has no statute altering the common-law duties of a person who voluntarily renders assistance.
questions.call: Is the hiker likely liable for the additional injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the hiker had no initial duty to rescue the stranger.
choices.rationale: Although there may have been no initial duty to act, a person who voluntarily undertakes aid must exercise reasonable care in performing it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the hiker voluntarily undertook assistance and performed it without reasonable care.
choices.rationale: The negligent undertaking increased the stranger's harm and supports liability for the additional injury.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the hiker acted with gross negligence or recklessness.
choices.rationale: Absent a modifying statute, the ordinary reasonable-care standard applies to a voluntary undertaking.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because anyone who begins a rescue is strictly liable for every resulting injury.
choices.rationale: The doctrine imposes a duty of reasonable care, not strict liability.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a person who voluntarily undertakes a rescue owes reasonable care.
explanation.governingRule: A person generally has no duty to rescue a stranger, but one who voluntarily undertakes to render aid must exercise reasonable care and may be liable for increasing the risk of harm or causing additional injury through negligent performance.
explanation.application: The hiker chose to act and carelessly handled the stretcher, directly causing a new injury. The absence of an initial duty does not excuse negligent performance.
explanation.commonTrap: Stopping the analysis after concluding there was no duty to begin the rescue.
explanation.memoryAid: No duty to start may become a duty to use care once the rescue begins.

authorityNotes: Restatement (Second) of Torts section 323; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.stem: A beverage manufacturer used extensive quality-control procedures when producing sealed bottles of sparkling water. Because of an isolated malfunction, a small metal fragment entered one bottle during production. The bottle then passed through ordinary distribution channels without alteration. A consumer bought the bottle from a grocery store, opened it normally, and was injured by the metal fragment while drinking the water. The consumer sued the manufacturer under strict products liability. The manufacturer established that it had exercised reasonable care in designing and operating its production system.
questions.call: Is the manufacturer likely liable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the bottle departed from its intended condition and the defect caused the consumer's injury.
choices.rationale: The metal fragment is a manufacturing defect, and the product reached the consumer without substantial change.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the manufacturer exercised reasonable care in producing the bottle.
choices.rationale: Strict products liability does not require proof that the manufacturer acted negligently.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the consumer did not purchase the bottle directly from the manufacturer.
choices.rationale: Privity is generally not required for strict products liability against a commercial manufacturer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the consumer proves that a safer alternative bottle design was economically feasible.
choices.rationale: A reasonable alternative design is not required to prove a manufacturing defect, which concerns departure from the intended design.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a manufacturer is strictly liable for a manufacturing defect despite exercising reasonable care.
explanation.governingRule: A commercial seller is subject to strict products liability when a defective product reaches a foreseeable user without substantial change and the defect causes injury. A manufacturing defect exists when the particular product departs from its intended design.
explanation.application: The metal fragment resulted from an isolated production malfunction, remained in the sealed bottle, and caused injury during normal use. Due care and lack of direct sale do not defeat the claim.
explanation.commonTrap: Requiring negligence or a safer alternative design for every products-liability theory.
explanation.memoryAid: Manufacturing defect asks whether this unit differed from what the manufacturer intended.

authorityNotes: Restatement (Second) of Torts section 402A and manufacturing-defect doctrine; attorney verification required

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication.
